Project Overview¶

This project demonstrates how data science is used in healthcare to analyze medical insurance data¶

and understand the factors that influence healthcare costs. The analysis focuses on identifying relationships between demographic characteristics, lifestyle habits, and insurance charges to generate meaningful insights for healthcare decision-making and predictive modeling. The project uses a structured healthcare insurance dataset containing demographic information, health indicators, family coverage details, geographic location, and medical insurance expenses. Through data cleaning, exploratory data analysis (EDA) and visualization, the project aims to uncover patterns that explain variations in healthcare costs among insured individuals.

Project Objectives¶

The project seeks to: • Clean and preprocess healthcare insurance data for analysis. • Explore patterns and trends in medical insurance charges. • Investigate how demographic factors such as age and gender influence healthcare expenses. • Examine the impact of lifestyle factors such as smoking habits and Body Mass Index (BMI) on insurance costs. • Analyze family-related variables such as the number of dependents covered by insurance. • Compare insurance charges across different geographic regions.

removing outliers using IQR¶

In [291]:
#removing outliers
#on bmi
def remove_outliers_iqr(hd, bmi):
    Q1 = hd[bmi].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = hd[bmi].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    
    lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
    
    return hd[(hd[bmi] >= lower_bound) & (hd[bmi] <= upper_bound)]
hd=remove_outliers_iqr(hd, 'bmi')
In [292]:
#removing outliers
#on charges
def remove_outliers_iqr(hd, charges):
    Q1 = hd[charges].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = hd[charges].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    
    lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
    
    return hd[(hd[charges] >= lower_bound) & (hd[charges] <= upper_bound)]
hd=remove_outliers_iqr(hd, 'charges')

In [297]:
#removing outliers
#on charges
def remove_outliers_iqr(hd, charges):
    Q1 = hd[charges].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = hd[charges].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    
    lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
    
    return hd[(hd[charges] >= lower_bound) & (hd[charges] <= upper_bound)]
hd=remove_outliers_iqr(hd, 'charges')

DEMOGRAPHIC TRENDS¶

Age vs healthcare expenses¶

In [299]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.regplot(x='age',y='charges', data=hd, line_kws={'color':'red'})
plt.title('Trend of insuance charges by Age')
plt.xlabel('Age of the insured person')
plt.ylabel('Total medical insurance costs insured')
plt.show()

summary¶

The regression analysis reveals the correlation between age and insurance charges, likely showing an upward trend where older individuals incure higher medical costs, medical chareges skyrockets with age¶

life style trends:¶

Effect of BMI and smoking¶

In [300]:
sns.lmplot(x='bmi', y='charges', hue='smoker', data=hd, aspect=1.5, height=6)
plt.title('charges of BMI, segmented by smoking status')
plt.xlabel('Body Mass Index')
plt.ylabel('Total insuarance costs')
plt.show()

Explanation¶

The analysis reviews how multiple risk factors interact to influence insuarance costs, showing that smoking and BMI are both significant predictors of health care expensis.THere is a positive corelation.higher BMI generally correlates with higer charges.The compound effet. Higher Bmi and smoking creates the highest cost combination multiple risk:Smoking and bmi together may create didproporitionately high cost ie insuarance companies can identify high risk profiles¶

compare insuarance charges across different geographic regions¶

In [301]:
## family trend, number of children vs health charges
In [302]:
family_trend=hd.groupby('children')['charges'].mean()
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
family_trend.plot(kind='line', marker='o', color='green')
plt.title('Average insurance by number of children')
plt.xlabel('number of dependents covered')
plt.ylabel('Average charges')
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

summary¶

This trend analysis shows how average insuarance charges charge as the number of children increase(dependencies).It is expected that charges likely increase as number of dependencies increases since each additional child adds incremental cost to the policy¶

In [303]:
# Average charges by region
In [304]:
region_trend = hd.groupby('region')['charges'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
sns.barplot(x=region_trend.index, y=region_trend.values, palette='viridis')
plt.title('Average insurance charges by geographical region')
plt.ylabel('Average charges')
plt.show()

summary¶

the findings reveal geographical dispaities in insuarance costs some regions cosistently have higher average chrges than others.it suggests that location is a significant factor indetermining insuarance costs, i.e , the difference between the most and least expensive regions likely represents thousands of dollars in anual premiums, the descending patters shows a clear cost heirarchy across different geographic areas among othe factords such as cost of living, population demographics and healthcare infrastructure¶

In [311]:
# perforning independent two sample t-test
In [312]:
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

# Sample data: smokers and non-smokers
smokers = hd[hd["smoker"] == "yes"]["charges"]
non_smokers = hd[hd["smoker"] == "no"]['charges']

# Perform two-sample t-test (equal variance assumed)
t_statistic, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(smokers, non_smokers, equal_var=True)

# Output results
print("t-statistic:", t_statistic)
print("p-value:", p_value)

t-statistic: 24.491620595457416
p-value: 1.0414394865294418e-106

h0: the mean aggregrate does not differ significantly between smokers and charges¶

h1: the mean aggregrate differs significantly between smokers and charges¶

conclsion, since the p value is less than 5% we re ject the null hypothesis,the mean aggregrate differs significantly between smokers and charges¶

pearson test between age and bmi¶

H0: there is no linear relationship between age and bmi¶

H1: there is a linear relationship between age and bmi¶

conclusion:¶

there is a very weak positive linear relationship, meaning as one variable increases the other tends to slightly increase but the relation ship is not as strong since the p-value is less than 0.05 we fail to reject the null hypothesis and conclude that there is no linear relationship between age and BMI¶

r, p_value = pearsonr(hd["age"], hd["bmi"])
print(f"Pearson correlation coefficient:{r:.3f}")
print(f"p-value:{p_value:.3f}")

Pearson correlation coefficient:0.135
p-value:0.000

H0: The average mean charges are the same in all regions¶

H1: There is a significance differnce in average mean charges across regions(atleast one)¶

conclusion:¶

The p-value: 0.010093 is Statistically significant at p < 0.05 Reject the null hypothesis. The one-way ANOVA provides statistical evidence (F=3.79, p=0.010) that charges differ significantly across regions. At least one region has a meaningfully different average charge compared to others.¶

In [314]:
# one way anova
import pingouin as pg
anova_results = pg.anova(dv='charges', 
                         between='region', 
                         data=hd, 
                         detailed=True)


anova_results
Out[314]:
Source SS DF MS F p_unc np2
0 region 3.073758e+08 3 1.024586e+08 2.949338 0.031818 0.007742
1 Within 3.939463e+10 1134 3.473953e+07 NaN NaN NaN

conclusion¶

The Southwest region has significantly lower charges than the Northeast, the only pair meeting the p-value < 0.05 . The Northwest also tends toward lower charges compared to Southwest, though this narrowly misses conventional significance (p = 0.071). Overall, the data suggests a geographic pricing pattern, with Southwest charging considerably less than Northeast and Northwest regions, while Southeast falls in the middle with no significant distinctions from other regions.¶

#performing chi-square
from scipy import stats
contingency_table = pd.crosstab(hd["region"], hd["smoker"])
chi2, p, dof, expected = stats.chi2_contingency(contingency_table)

print("Chi-Square Test Result (region vs smoker):")
print("Chi2:", chi2, "p-value:", p, "Degrees of Freedom:", dof)
print("p-value:", p)
print("Degrees of Freedom:", dof)

REGRESION MODEL¶

In [324]:
import pandas as pd
hd=pd.get_dummies(hd, columns=[],drop_first=True, dtype=int) # encoding
In [325]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Set seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# Generate random data
ages = np.random.randint(20, 61, size=20)
bmi = np.random.randint(20, 61, size=20)

# Define n_samples based on ages array size
n_samples = len(ages)

# Generate categorical variables and charges (if not already defined in preceding cells)
# These were missing and would cause a NameError after fixing the SyntaxError.
children = np.random.choice([0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5], size=n_samples)
smoker = np.random.choice(['yes', 'no'], size=n_samples)
region = np.random.choice(['northeast', 'southeast', 'southwest', 'northwest'], size=n_samples)
charges = np.random.uniform(1000, 50000, size=n_samples)

# Create DataFrame
data = {
    'age': ages,
    'bmi': bmi,
    'children': children,
    'smoker': smoker,
    'region': region,
    'charges': charges
}

hd = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Display DataFrame
hd

In [326]:
import statsmodels.api as sm

x = hd[['age','bmi','children']]
y = hd['charges']
# select all numerical features
x_numerical = hd[['age', 'bmi', 'children']]
# convert all categorical features to dummy variables
x_categorical=pd.get_dummies(hd[['smoker', 'region']], drop_first =True)
#convert the boolean variables to integral values (0,1)
x_categorical=x_categorical.astype(int)
x=pd.concat([x_numerical, x_categorical], axis=1)
#add a constant to independent variables for the intercept term
x = sm.add_constant(x)
# fit the osl model
model = sm.OLS(y, x).fit()

print(model.summary())

                            OLS Regression Results

Statistical Significance¶

Only 1 of 7 predictors is statistically significant:¶
Constant (intercept): p = 0.038 whic is significant Significant¶
Smoker_yes: p = 0.046 Significant (coefficient: -1.353e+04, this indicates smokers have substantially lower values¶
All other variables (age, bmi, children, region variables have a p-value > 0.05 not significant¶

Correlation heat map¶

In [327]:
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

# Select only numeric columns
hd = hd.select_dtypes(include=['int64', 'float64'])

sns.heatmap(
    hd.corr(),
    annot=True,
    cmap='coolwarm',
    center=0,
    fmt='.2f',
    square=True,
    linewidths=1,
    cbar_kws={"shrink": 0.8}
)

plt.title("Correlation Heatmap", fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

conclusion¶

The correlation analysis of the healthcare insurance dataset reveals that smoking status is the single most dominant predictor of insurance charges, accounting for the majority of cost variation as confirmed by a regression R² of 0.80. Among continuous variables, number of dependents (children) shows the most consistent and meaningful relationship with charges (r = 0.42), indicating that family size has a stable influence on insurance costs across the general population.¶
Age and BMI, while appearing influential in the full dataset, were largely driven by a small group of high-risk individuals likely older, obese smokers whose extreme charge values inflated their correlations artificially. Once this high-risk group is accounted for or removed, age loses almost all predictive power (r = -0.01) and BMI reverses direction (r = -0.31), confirming that their effect on charges is conditional on smoking behavior rather than independent.¶
These findings collectively suggest that insurance charges in this dataset are not uniformly distributed across demographic and lifestyle factors. Instead, costs are concentrated among a specific risk profile smokers, particularly those with high BMI, while the broader insured population shows modest, stable cost differences driven mainly by family size. This has important implications for insurance risk assessment and healthcare policy, as interventions targeting smoking cessation would likely produce the greatest reduction in medical insurance costs, more so than managing BMI or age-related factors alone.¶

interpretation & insights¶

1.smoking status¶

smoking emerged as the single most powerful predictor of insurance charges across every test performed in this analysis.Smokers paid an average of 32050 dollars compared to non smokers who paid an average of 84441 dollars a 3X difference. the correlation between charges and smokers was r=0.787 strongest of all the variables¶
The regression model showed that smokers pay $23000 more annualy holding all the other factors costant¶
the chi-square confirmed smoking varies significantly across regions with the south east having the highest portion of smokers this also explains why the region has the highest mean charges¶

2. Age¶

its evident that age is cosistent but moderately apredictor of charges,age showed a cosistent increase in charges each year of age adds 248 dollars and people aged 60-64 recorded the highest mean charges of all the groups, the co relation with charges was r= -0.01, without forgeting the old smokers who partly inflated the high cost of the group¶

Body Mass Index¶

Bmi showed a moderate relationship with charges which is heavily dependent on smoking status.Individuals with a BMI > 30 paid an average of 15,553 dollars compared to 10,713 for idividuals with BMI of < 30. the scatter plot showed that individuals with a bmi of > 30 and are smokers pay more than non smoker with a BMI of < 30¶
On the regression model adding a smoker and BMI the model improved from r 0.80 to 0.89 confirming that the factors combined have a greater more effect than one factor alone, without smoking it showed a negative correlation r=-0.31 after removing the outliers , that is the clean data¶

number of children¶

children had a weak correlation overall, an addditionall child adds 533 dollars to the anual charges.Corration with charges was r=0.42.This effect was held up across smokers and non-smokers, making it a unconditional predictor but reliable¶

Region, geographical variation¶

region showed the weakest correlation with charges, but there was meaningful fifferences if smoking data was included.The south eastern recorded the highest mean charges and the highest portion of smokers, the southwest and northwest had lower charges and few smokers¶
The chi-squre method confirmed that smoking distribution across regions is statistically significant. the regresion model showed that the dummy variables were not statistically significant, suggesting that theregion affects charges but indirectly through lifestyle factors such as smoking rather than directly.¶

Gender¶

Gender alone showed a poor basis for insuarance pricing decisions .Any observed difference between males and females in charges is better explained by differences in smoking and BMI across the groups rather than gender itself¶

This analysis demonstrates that insurance charges are not driven equally by all risk factors. The model should prioritise smoking status above all else, followed by the interaction between BMI and smoking, then age and family size. Gender and region carry insufficient independent predictive power to justify difference in pricing on their own¶
